paper_title: Taste-aware music retrieval from audio embeddings

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Crossmodal correspondences between sound and taste are well established in psychology and neuroscience, but largely absent from content-based multimedia retrieval. We formalise taste-from-audio prediction as a content-based music information retrieval benchmark over a perceptually validated multi-source corpus, comparing ten frozen audio encoders from the four HEAR families under a shared multi-task regression head, with gated late-fusion as a configurable variant. In order to assess the effectiveness of the models, we compute absolute error and rank correlation. The strongest systems predict the five tastes within a macro RMSE of 0.134; on held-out real music their error is less than half a single rater's deviation from the consensus (RMSE 0.13 vs. 0.28), so the model tracks the group consensus more closely than an average human rater, and well below the previous state of the art baseline (0.219). On absolute error the encoders are statistically flat, with a single VGGish matching the best fusion, but gated late-fusion's advantage is confined to rank correlation (macro Pearson r 0.724 vs. 0.666). Operationalised as a content-based retrieval index, the predicted taste space ranks a 309-item pool far more faithfully than a CLAP-text baseline, which sits at chance; ridge probes and an audio-bandstop knockout read the strongest representations against documented sound-taste correspondences.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Taste-aware music retrieval from audio embeddings Matteo Spanio University of Padua, Padua, Italy spanio@dei.unipd.it Antonio Rod`a University of Padua, Padua, Italy roda@dei.unipd.it 6 2 0 2 ] . [ 1 6 9 2 3 0 . 7 0 6 2 : r AbstractCrossmodal correspondences between sound and taste are well established in psychology and neuroscience, but largely absent from content-based multimedia retrieval. We formalise taste-from-audio prediction as content-based music information retrieval benchmark over perceptually validated multi-source corpus, comparing ten frozen audio encoders from the four HEAR families under shared multi-task regression head, with gated late-fusion as configurable variant. In order to assess the effectiveness of the models, we compute absolute error and rank correlation. The strongest systems predict the five tastes within macro RMSE of 0.134; on held-out real music their error is less than half single raters deviation from the consensus (RMSE 0.13 vs. 0.28), so the model tracks the group consensus more closely than an average human rater, and well below the previous state of the art baseline (0.219). On absolute error the encoders are statistically flat, with single VGGISH matching the best fusion, but gated late-fusions advantage is confined to rank correlation (macro Pearson 0.724 vs. 0.666). Operationalised as content-based retrieval index, the predicted taste space ranks 309-item pool far more faithfully than CLAP-text baseline, which sits at chance; ridge probes and an audio-bandstop knockout read the strongest representations against documented soundtaste correspondences. Index Termsmusic information retrieval, crossmodal learning, content-based retrieval, sonic seasoning I. INTRODUCTION Crossmodal correspondences between sound and taste are one of the clearest examples of stable associations between audition and the chemical senses. High pitch, consonance, and bright timbre are repeatedly associated with sweetness, while lower pitch, roughness, and darker timbre shift listeners toward bitterness and sourness [1][3]. These effects already motivate sonic-seasoning applications in restaurants, advertising, and multisensory design [4], yet they remain peripheral to mainstream music information retrieval (MIR) and multimedia benchmarking. This gap matters for content-based multimedia indexing. If taste judgments can be predicted from audio in reproducible way, they become new semantic axis for organising collections, querying music beyond genre and mood, and recommendation scenarios such as similar track but sweeter. The task also creates an unusual test-bed for explainable multimedia is useful only if it scores well and if learning: model its behaviour can be compared with empirical findings from psychology and neuroscience. Taste tagging from audio is not completely novel task. Guedes et al. introduced the Taste & Affect Music Database [5]; Rodriguez fine-tuned five separate Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) regressors, one per taste, on curated Fig. 1. Proposed model architecture. One or more frozen audio encoders produce per-encoder embeddings that are concatenated and re-weighted by learned per-encoder gate; the gated representation feeds shared two-layer MLP whose sigmoid head outputs 5-D taste vector in [0, 1]5. 257-song soundtracks corpus and used them to label the FMA corpus at scale [6]; Spanio et al. extended the corpus, validated the labels perceptually, and explored generative variants [7], [8]. Prior work established the feasibility of taste prediction from audio, with evaluation centred on direct regression error. The present paper expands it into broader content-based MIR setting; we make three contributions. We formalise taste-from-audio as content-based MIR benchmark over perceptually validated multi-source corpus, with frozen-encoder protocol across ten encoders covering the four HEAR families [9], in singleencoder and gated late-fusion forms (five-seed means). Read for rating replacement, the best systems reach macro RMSE of 0.134; on held-out real music this is less than half single raters error to the consensus (RMSE 0.13 vs. 0.28) and far below the previous baseline [6] (0.219). single VGGISH already reaches this error; gated late-fusion adds only rank correlation (r 0.724 vs. 0.666). We operationalise the predicted taste space as contentbased retrieval index: over 309-item pool with tasteprofile queries leads every metric, while generic CLAP-text [10] baseline collapses to chance, to our knowledge the first retrieval evaluation of tasteconditioned music indexing. [8] it We pair the benchmark with psychophysics-grounded interpretability layer, ridge probes to nine spectral descriptors and cross-encoder audio-bandstop knockout, reading the strongest representations against documented soundtaste correspondences [1][3]. II. RELATED WORKS III. METHODOLOGY A. Sound-taste correspondences and sonic seasoning Crossmodal correspondences between auditory parameters and basic tastes have been documented for over decade. Psychophysical work maps pitch, consonance, timbre, and tempo onto the four canonical tastes: high pitch, consonance, and bright timbre with sweetness, low pitch, roughness, and darker timbres with bitterness [1][3], [11], sweet and bitter showing the largest effects, with replications confirming the instrument-to-taste mapping [12][15]. Sound also modulates perceived intensity, texture, and emotional appraisal of food and drink [16][18], grounding the sonic-seasoning program in restaurants, advertising, and multisensory design [4]. These are crossmodal correspondences, population-wide soundtaste associations distinct from synaesthesias idiosyncratic crossactivation in small minority [1]; we model the former, shared across listeners. [19] threads. Compositional: Computational work on soundtaste has developed along four introduced musicgeneration algorithm built on tastemusic correspondences. Stimulus norming: [5] produced 100-track music database normed for sweet/bitter/sour/salty. Discriminative: [6] finetuned five separate Audio Spectrogram Transformer regressors on curated soundtracks corpus and used them to label the FMA dataset [20] at scale. Generative and dataset-level: Spanio et al. surveyed the multimodal-generative-AI design space for sound and taste [21], fine-tuned MusicGEN [22] on taste-prompted corpus [7], and produced unified music taste corpus validated by 49-participant perceptual study [8]. The present paper sits in the discriminative thread, replacing the five-head per-taste architecture with single multi-task head over frozen encoders. B. Audio representations, explainability, and retrieval Modern audio representations span supervised event models [23][25], self-supervised speech and music models [26] [28], multimodal and codec-based models [10], [29], and [30]. Benchmarks such as music-pretrained representations HEAR [9] aggregate tasks across these families but exclude sensory dimensions like taste; [31] reaches similar conclusion for music emotion. Closest to us, [32] predicts 19 perceived-semantic axes of functional sounds by feature extraction and ensembles, and [33] predicts perceived musical expression on advertising stimuli, both from audio features rather than human-rated taste labels. For explainability, ridge probes over embeddings [32] and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) [34] are standard probes of what an embedding linearly preserves; we adopt both. For attribute-guided retrieval, [35] showed that frozen music embeddings support attribute-controlled search through learned transformation; we treat their setup as the downstream test bed and report static retrieval-by-taste-profile baseline robust at our corpus scale. A. Dataset and task Experiments use the unified musictaste corpus of [8], which aggregates audiotaste annotations from three sources, through fixed split column that we do not reshuffle (269 train + 68 val + 40 test clips): the real-music perceptually validated subset of [8] (49 raters, 20 dishes), the MusicGen-generated subset of [7] (436 raters, 100 songs), and the soundtracks survey corpus of [6] (257 songs). Targets are five normalised taste intensities (sweet, bitter, salty, sour, spicy): the first four are the canonical basic tastes whose crossmodal mappings to sound are characterised in psychophysics [1][3], [36], while spicy is trigeminal/chemesthetic axis rated alongside them in the source studies, though less constrained by prior evidence. Each clip carries partial observation masks (the MusicGengenerated subset omits spicy), so we train with masked MSE loss to avoid phantom-zero signals on unobserved cells. Because subjective labels carry corpus-specific noise [31], [32], we report metrics ceiling-aware: the test set mixes 20 real-music [8] and 20 MusicGen-generated items [7], whose leave-one-out inter-rater Pearson ceilings differ substantially ( 0.60 vs. 0.25), so each model is read against the noise floor of its source. B. Models architecture The model is frozen audio encoder followed by small multi-task head  (Fig. 1)  . For any 15 audio clip x, encoder fθ produces an embedding fθ(x) RD that is mean-pooled along the time axis and cached on disk; two-layer MLP (hidden size 256, ReLU, dropout 0.2) with final sigmoid maps the embedding to 5-D taste vector in [0, 1]5. Frozenencoder + linear/MLP probes are the standard recipe for benchmarking pretrained audio representations on small downstream corpora [9], [32]; we adopt it for reproducibility and because cached embeddings keep an encoder sweep tractable at this scale. The sigmoid bounds the output and prevents the out-of-range predictions that the previous SOTAs five unbounded linear regressors produce on the same data [6]. Masked MSE keeps the partial labels of the MusicGen subset from collapsing to phantom zeros, and the multi-task head exploits the strong inter-target correlations in normed taste music corpora [5], [8]. Training. AdamW, learning rate 103, weight decay 104, batch size 32, up to 50 epochs with early stopping (patience 10) on the validation macro r. Every reported metric for the multi-task MLP head and its fusion variants is the mean over five seeds ({11, 22, 33, 44, 55}); the encoder cache is computed once and reused across seeds. C. Audio encoders and fusion The benchmark spans ten frozen audio encoders that together cover the four families surveyed in [9]: supervised event/scene models (VGGish [23], PANNs [24], AST [25]), self-supervised speech and music models (HuBERT [26], MERT [27], Omar-RQ [28]), multimodal and codec-based models (CLAP [10], EnCodec [29]), and music-pretrained representations (MULE [30]), with MFCC as the DSP floor. Frozen encoders are the default regime because the task is low-N and cached embeddings make broad comparison reproducible. We also evaluate the previous SOTA ast-5head-ref [6], five fine-tuned AST regressors (one per taste) on raw audio, the natural reference for our single multi-task head. the concatenated embeddings: Gated late-fusion. To combine encoders we add learned gate over each per-encoder slice is scaled by sigmoid weight before the gate trained jointly with the head the MLP head, while encoders stay frozen. We pick fusion candidates by complementarity rather following the ensemble logic of [32] and the cross-objective combination of [35], and evaluate seven configurations over the four strongest encoders: the pairs AST+VGGish, AST+MULE, CLAP+VGGish, VGGish+MULE, CLAP+MULE, triple AST+VGGish+MULE, the four-way CLAP+AST+VGGish+MULE. than raw rank, and the D. Explainability and retrieval probes We attach two analysis layers to the strongest models. First, psychoacoustic ridge probes: 5-fold cross-validated regression from each cached embedding to nine acoustic descriptors (spectral centroid, rolloff, bandwidth, flatness, ZCR, RMS, tempo, harmonic-to-noise ratio, contrast), the cues routinely linked to crossmodal taste percepts (bright/sharp spectra to sweet, low-frequency energy to bitter) [1], [2], [11], [13], [15]; an embedding that still linearly preserves them has not discarded what the literature predicts taste-aware model should use. Descriptors come from librosa [37] (default 2048/512 window/hop); features and targets are standardized on the train fold, ridge α = 1.0, and held-out R2 reports what each embedding preserves [32]. Second, cross-encoder audio-bandstop knockout: zero-phase 4th-order Butterworth bandstop (forwardbackward) at each of eight mel bands over 08 kHz, RMS-renormalised so loudness is not confound, then re-encoded; the per-(band, taste) Pearson drop localises which bands the encoder relies on, in the spirit of CKA [34] and frequency-band ablation [32] but applied at the input.

sections.title: For the downstream task we index test
sections.content: items by their predicted 5-D taste vectors and rank them against five named taste-profile queries, the same regime as attributeconditioned music retrieval [35] with taste replacing mood. The queries are not handcrafted: each is the mean perceptually rated taste vector of foods with that dominant taste in the dataset of [8] (sweet for dessert, bittersweet for dark-chocolate, salty for umami-savory, sour for citrus, spicy for chili-burn). Items rank by Euclidean distance; precision@k binarises ground-truth distance at its median (applied uniformly to every system), complemented by test-only Spearman ρ and test-vs-distractor AUC that uses no distractor labels. IV. EXPERIMENTS We evaluate three linked research questions, each anchored to one subsection of the Results. RQ1. Under unified multi-task head with masked MSE, which encoder family transfers best to taste prediction, and does late fusion across complementary families improve macro Pearson over the previous SOTA [6]? RQ2. Do the strongest systems preserve interpretable spectral cues consistent with the crossmodal psychophysics literature [1][3], [36], and can the models reliance on specific input frequency bands be localised per taste? RQ3. Does the predicted 5-D taste space support contentbased retrieval against principled taste-profile queries [35], and does it outperform random ranking and text-based retrieval through generic audiotext embedding [10]? a) Metrics.: For RQ1 we report per-target Pearson r, macro Pearson r, macro MAE, and macro RMSE on the heldout test split, with per-source breakdown so absolute scores can be read against the source-specific inter-rater ceilings of [7], [8], reporting style consistent with how [31] and [32] recommend handling subjective-label benchmarks. The metric we foreground follows the use case. Because the intended application is to replace or augment human rater, the error metrics (RMSE, MAE, in rating units) are primary and are compared against single human raters leave-one-out error to the consensus, computed on the real-music subset from the 49-rater study [8]; macro Pearson is foundamental as well, reported for comparability with prior work [5], [6] and as the rank metric for the retrieval task (RQ3). Multi-seed configurations are reported as mean std over five seeds. For RQ2 we report held-out R2 of the psychoacoustic ridge probes (5-fold CV, α = 1) and per-taste when each mel band is masked at the input. For RQ3 we use the five named queries described in V-C, derived from the foodstimulus dataset of [8], and report @k (k {5, 10, 20}), test-only Spearman ρ, and test-vs-distractor AUC against three reference points: (i) random-ranking baseline, (ii) CLAPtext retrieval baseline [10] on natural-language paraphrases of each query, and (iii) the previous five-AST-heads SOTA [6] ranking by its own predicted taste vectors. We do not include ground-truth oracle in the table because, under the precision protocol used here, it trivially scores 1.0 on every and carries no diagnostic information. b) Significance.: Encoder rankings are confirmed by paired-bootstrap tests over the test split with 2000 resamples; only the rankings explicitly described as ties fail to clear < 0.05. We retain source-aware reporting throughout because aggregate numbers alone hide label-noise effects on the MusicGen-generated subset [7]. V. RESULTS A. Encoder benchmark and fusion Table benchmarks ten frozen encoders under our shared the SOTA ast-5head-ref [6] multi-task head against and seven late-fusion variants; every learned configuration is reported as five-seed mean to keep the comparison stable on TABLE HELD-OUT TEST-SET METRICS FOR TEN FROZEN ENCODERS, THE PREVIOUS SOTA FIVE-AST-HEADS BASELINE [6], AND SEVEN GATED LATE-FUSION VARIANTS. PER-TARGET PEARSON IN THE FIVE MIDDLE COLUMNS; MACRO r, MAE, RMSE IN THE RIGHTMOST BLOCK. EVERY LEARNED CONFIGURATION (SINGLE ENCODERS AND FUSIONS) IS THE MEAN OVER FIVE SEEDS {11, 22, 33, 44, 55}; T-5H D-R IS FIXED EXTERNAL BASELINE EVALUATED ONCE. ROWS ARE SORTED BY MACRO WITHIN EACH BLOCK, AND BOLD MARKS THE COLUMN-BEST VALUE ACROSS THE TABLE. sweet bitter salty sour spicy Macro MAE RMSE Method MULE [30] VGGish [23] CLAP [10] AST [25] PANNs [24] HuBERT [26] EnCodec [29] MFCC (DSP floor) MERT [27] Omar-RQ [28] +0.641 +0.831 +0.645 +0.609 +0.610 +0.870 +0.728 +0.367 +0.578 +0.787 +0.666 +0.798 +0.607 +0.651 +0.598 +0.564 +0.824 +0.709 +0.580 +0.615 +0.673 +0.816 +0.608 +0.533 +0.528 +0.741 +0.749 +0.600 +0.528 +0.462 +0.571 +0.725 +0.591 +0.389 +0.409 +0.621 +0.708 +0.290 +0.577 +0.396 +0.708 +0.439 +0.477 +0.519 +0.139 +0.450 +0.523 +0.562 +0.438 0.357 ast-5head-ref (SOTA, [6]) +0.740 +0.563 +0.468 +0.325 +0.682 fusion VGGish+MULE +0.851 +0.756 +0.643 +0.657 +0.715 fusion CLAP+VGGish +0.836 +0.645 +0.637 +0.652 +0.788 fusion AST+VGGish+MULE +0.844 +0.750 +0.659 +0.697 +0.600 fusion CLAP+AST+VGGish+MULE +0.839 +0.709 +0.651 +0.664 +0.673 fusion CLAP+MULE +0.813 +0.685 +0.665 +0.648 +0.696 fusion AST+MULE +0.580 +0.844 +0.728 +0.652 +0.647 fusion AST+VGGish (gated) +0.606 +0.812 +0.743 +0.613 +0.666 0.667 0.666 0.664 0.658 0.632 0.616 0.537 0.518 0.456 0.323 0.556 0.724 0.712 0.710 0.707 0.701 0.690 0. 0.116 0.109 0.114 0.116 0.119 0.121 0.128 0.142 0.142 0.141 0.175 0.111 0.109 0.112 0.110 0.114 0.113 0.113 0.146 0.134 0.142 0.143 0.146 0.150 0.159 0.181 0.173 0.169 0.219 0.134 0.135 0.137 0.134 0.139 0.140 0. the 40-item test set. We read macro RMSE and MAE as the primary metrics: the target use is to stand in for or augment human taste ratings, where what matters is how far predicted rating sits from the true one in rating units, and the loss is masked MSE on [0, 1] targets. Macro Pearson is secondary, tracking rank quality for the content-based retrieval/indexing use case of V-C. (i) Four encoders from three families tie at the top of the aggregate (MULE = 0.667, VGGish 0.666, CLAP 0.664, AST 0.658, 0.009 spread within their seed noise), but their per-taste profiles differ: VGGish leads sweet, spicy, and bitter yet collapses on salty, where CLAP and MULE are most reliable and AST leads sour (Table I). No single family dominates across tastes, and this complementarity, also reported by [30], [32], is what the fusion exploits. (ii) On absolute error the encoders are flat: single VGGISH already reaches the lowest RMSE (0.134) and MAE (0.109) in the table, which no fusion improves on, so for rating replacement one frozen encoder suffices. Gated late-fusion instead buys rank correlation: the strongest pair, VGGISH+MULE, lifts macro to 0.724 0.020 (+0.057 over MULE) at the same RMSE (0.134), with CLAP+VGGISH (0.712) and the triple AST+VGGISH+MULE (0.710) close behind. The seven fusions span only 0.6880.724 in macro r, mostly within one anothers seed bands, so no pairing is decisively best, and pertarget leadership stays spread across them (Table I), with the single VGGISH still holding sweet (+0.870). Stacking all four encoders does not help; complementarity matters more than count. (iii) Every learned configuration matches or beats the SOTA on macro RMSE (best 0.134 vs. 0.219), so the five-head fine-tuned design of [6] carries clear absolute-error cost. a) Decomposing the gap.: Table II attributes the gain over [6] to three cumulative changes (five-seed means). The frozen-encoder + per-taste-MLP + masked-MSE + sigmoid swap closes most of the RMSE gap (0.219 0.143); collapsing the five heads to one shared multi-task head holds RMSE and (0.663 0.658) at 5 smaller head; gated fusion TABLE II CUMULATIVE ABLATION FROM THE PREVIOUS SOTA [6] (ROW 1) TO THE BEST GATED FUSION (ROW 4). EACH ROW STACKS THE CHANGE DESCRIBED IN ITS LEFTMOST COLUMN ON TOP OF THE ROW ABOVE; ROWS 24 ARE FIVE-SEED MEANS EVALUATED ON THE SAME HELD-OUT TEST SPLIT (ROW 4 FUSES THE BEST PAIR FROM TABLE I). BOLD MARKS THE COLUMN-BEST VALUE. configuration RMSE MAE macro SOTA [6] (fine-tuned AST, unbounded MSE) + frozen AST, per-taste MLP, masked MSE, sigmoid + shared multi-task head (single AST encoder) + gated late-fusion VGGish+MULE 0.219 0.143 0.143 0.134 0.175 0.115 0.116 0.111 0.556 0.663 0.658 0.724 adds the rest ( 0.134). Loss and output redesign therefore dominate over architecture (the first swap alone is 0.076 of the 0.085 RMSE gain), consistent with HEARs finding that frozen-encoder benchmarks are sensitive to probe and loss [9]; the gain transfers across AST, VGGish, MULE, and CLAP, so it is not single-encoder artefact. b) Per-source breakdown.: The 40-item test set splits into 20 real-music [8] and 20 MusicGen-generated items [7] with different inter-rater agreement (Table III). The single-rater leave-one-out ceiling on real music is r1 0.60 and, in rating units, MAE 0.227/RMSE 0.280; the best systems real-music error (MAE 0.10, RMSE 0.13) is less than half of that, so frozen-encoder model estimates the group rating more closely than an average human rater, the core evidence that it can stand in for or be pooled with human ratings. The SOTA leads the real-music rank metrics (r = 0.767, ρ = 0.751), consistent with its soundtrack training, but AST+VGGish takes both error metrics there (MAE 0.099, RMSE 0.130), so its correlation edge does not extend to absolute error. On the harder generated subset (r1 0.25) frozen AST leads three of four columns and VGGish+MULE takes RMSE (0.133), while SOTAs error is 2.5 ours: it underpredicts bitter, salty, and sour by 0.250.37 (its training corpus lacks MusicGens high-intensity prompts [7], [22]), whereas the frozenencoder systems see both sources at training. VGGish+MULE TABLE III SOURCE-AWARE METRICS ON THE TWO TEST SUBSETS (n = 20 EACH): MACRO PEARSON r, SPEARMAN ρ, MAE, AND RMSE. BOTTOM ROW: SINGLE HUMAN RATERS LEAVE-ONE-OUT (LOO) AGREEMENT WITH THE CONSENSUS, AS PEARSON (AVERAGED OVER THE PER-TASTE VALUES) AND AS ABSOLUTE ERROR. THE REAL-MUSIC ERROR CEILING (0.227/0.280) IS COMPUTED FROM THE 49-RATER STUDY; THE GENERATED SUBSET HAS NO COMPARABLE PER-RATER ERROR ESTIMATE, AND FAR LOWER AGREEMENT (KRIPPENDORFF α 0.10.2, ONLY 39% OF CLIPS HEARD AS THEIR PROMPT [7]). BOLD MARKS THE COLUMN-BEST VALUE AMONG THE MODELS. method real-music (n=20) ρ MAE RMSE MusicGen-generated (n=20) ρ MAE RMSE 0.613 AST 0.583 VGGISH 0.650 MULE 0.659 CLAP 0.767 SOTA [6] pair AST+VGGish 0.662 pair VGGish+MULE 0.694 triple AST+VGGish+MULE 0.678 0.645 0.605 0.653 0.678 0.751 0.674 0.689 0.691 0.112 0.110 0.116 0.115 0.112 0.099 0.111 0.116 0.137 0.132 0.145 0.140 0.142 0.130 0.132 0.138 0.559 0.486 0.433 0.504 0.454 0.537 0.539 0.429 0.581 0.482 0.420 0.519 0.491 0.555 0.564 0. 0.109 0.116 0.122 0.118 0.262 0.115 0.110 0.119 single human rater (LOO) 0.60 0.227 0.280 0.25 0.135 0.144 0.149 0.145 0.295 0.143 0.133 0. is the only system competitive on both subsets at once. c) Robustness and the small-N caveat.: Seed spread is small (on AST, five-seed = 0.658 0.010, RMSE 0.143 0.003) but dwarfed by sampling uncertainty on the 40-item test set: 2000-resample bootstrap gives the best fusions macro 95% CI roughly 0.18 wide ([0.64, 0.82]). The seven fusions therefore lie inside one anothers intervals, so their ordering is indicative, not significant; only the large gaps clear paired bootstrap at < 0.05 (any learned encoder over the SOTA, fusion over single encoder). The findings are stable to the head: linear ridge, kNN, heteroscedastic, and Gaussian-process heads stay within 0.05 macro of the MLP, the ridge probe nearly matching it, consistent with largely linear audiotaste relationship. B. Psychophysics-grounded strongest models work analysis explains why the a) Psychoacoustic probes: spectral structure predicts taste only loosely.: Fig. 2 reports held-out R2 for ridge probes from each embedding to nine psychoacoustic descriptors, the cue family the crossmodal literature ties to soundtaste associations [1][3]. CLAP leads every spectral column (R2 [0.78, 0.93]), so contrastive audiolanguage objective does not discard fine spectral structure; AST follows, then MERT, EnCodec, and MULE, strong on the brightness and bandwidth axes psychophysics ties to sweet and bitter [11], [15]. But decoding spectra and predicting taste track each other only loosely in both directions: MERT and EnCodec probe well yet rank near the bottom of Table I, while VGGish and PANNs predict taste well despite weak spectral R2. The encoders reach taste through partly-independent representations, which is what their gated fusion exploits; Omar-RQ and PANNs sit far below zero on most spectral columns, mirroring [32]. Loudness stays trivially decodable from codec objective (EnCodec RMS R2 = 0.987), and tempo is uniformly weak, consistent with it being the noisiest crossmodal axis [4], [36]. b) Bandstop knockout exposes different mechanisms.: second probe applies zero-phase 4th-order Butterworth bandstop (forwardbackward, RMS-renormalised) at each of TABLE IV CROSS-ENCODER AUDIO-BANDSTOP KNOCKOUT FOR THE FOUR STRONGEST ENCODERS. FOR EACH TASTE, THE INPUT FREQUENCY BAND WHOSE REMOVAL MOST CHANGES PER-TASTE PEARSON ON THE HELD-OUT TEST SPLIT. NEGATIVE r: BAND REMOVAL HURTS PERFORMANCE; POSITIVE (BAND none): THE MOST-AFFECTED BAND WAS NUISANCE. BOLD MARKS THE MOST-DAMAGING BAND REMOVAL PER ENCODER; CLAP HAS NONE (r 0.064 EVERYWHERE). taste AST VGGish CLAP MULE sweet 260610 Hz 0.039 260610 Hz 0.060 260610 Hz 0.038 260610 Hz 0.125 bitter 0260 Hz 0.129 0260 Hz 0.166 none +0.038 3.95.6 kHz 0.090 none +0.075 3.95.6 kHz 0.116 0260 Hz 0.064 1.11.8 kHz 0.294 salty +0.059 3.95.6 kHz 0.143 none none +0.043 1.82.7 kHz 0.149 sour +0.101 none spicy none +0.066 260610 Hz 0.024 1.82.7 kHz 0.264 eight mel-scale bands and measures the per-(band, Pearson drop after re-encoding (Table IV). taste) The four encoders rely on different mechanisms. AST in one or two narrow bands concentrates per-taste signal (sweet at 260610 Hz, bitter at sub-bass), with positive on salty, sour, and spicy (band-localised nuisance detectors, e.g. an anti-bass filter for spicy); VGGISH splits its dependencies between sub-bass (bitter, 0.166) and 3.95.6 kHz upper-mids (salty, sour); CLAP is band-robust (no removal exceeds 0.064), the strongest spectral decoder in Fig. 2; MULE is uniformly negative, concentrated in 13 kHz (salty 0.294, spicy 0.264). The macro-r benchmark hides this: the headline VGGISH+MULE pair combines two negativedependence encoders whose critical bands are disjoint (subbass+upper-mids vs. 13 kHz), exactly the complementarity its gating exploits. The mappings match the literature: bitterto-sub-bass tracks the replicated finding that low-pitched dark sounds elicit more bitterness [2], [15], [19], sweet-to-260 610 Hz sits in the bright-formant range reported as crossmodally sweet [11], [12], [38], and MULEs 13 kHz reliance overlaps the salty/sour region of [13], [14]. The bandstop is blunt probe [32], but the split is consistent across tastes and robust to filter-order perturbations. C. Retrieval by taste profile This is the second use case for the taste space, and the one closest to this venues content-based indexing focus: organising collection by predicted taste, where rank fidelity matters rather than absolute error, so Spearman ρ and Pearson are the metrics of record. We index test items by their predicted 5-D vectors and rank them by Euclidean distance to five taste-profile queries (dessert, dark-chocolate, umami-savory, citrus, chili-burn), mirroring [35]s attribute-guided setup for mood. Each query is the mean 5-D taste vector of foods with that dominant taste in the dataset of [8], preserving real-food structure (dessert carries sour from fruit; dark-chocolate pairs bitter and sweet) rather than collapsing to one-hot. a) Why the 309-item pool and three metrics.: On the 40item test set alone, median-binarised @k saturates (> 0.9 at = 5) for every reasonable predictor and cannot rank systems, known small-pool limitation [35]. We add the 269 training items as in-distribution distractors and report @k on the 309pool, test-only Spearman ρ on the 40 labeled items, and testFig. 2. Held-out R2 of 5-fold ridge probes from each cached embedding to nine psychoacoustic descriptors. Encoders are sorted by mean spectral R2; the horizontal line separates positivefrom negative-mean encoders, and the dashed vertical line separates spectral (left) from non-spectral (right) features. The color scale is clipped to [1, +1]; cells whose raw R2 falls outside this window are marked with . TABLE RETRIEVAL-BY-TASTE-PROFILE ON 309-ITEM POOL (40 TEST ITEMS + 269 TRAINING ITEMS AS IN-DISTRIBUTION DISTRACTORS), MEAN ACROSS THE FIVE FOOD-PROFILE QUERIES DERIVED FROM [8]. @k: PRECISION AT UNDER MEDIAN-BINARISED GROUND-TRUTH DISTANCE TO THE QUERY. ρ: SPEARMAN RANK CORRELATION BETWEEN PREDICTED AND GROUND-TRUTH DISTANCE ON THE 40 LABELED TEST ITEMS. AUC: ROC-AUC OF TEST-VS-DISTRACTOR DISCRIMINATION (0.5 = CHANCE). CLAP-TEXT [10] RANKS AUDIO BY COSINE SIMILARITY TO TEXTUAL PARAPHRASE OF EACH QUERY; IT SURFACES FULLY-OBSERVED ITEM IN THE TOP-k FOR AT MOST ONE QUERY, SO ITS @k IS UNDEFINED () AND ITS LOAD-BEARING ρ/AUC ARE REPORTED INSTEAD. BOLD MARKS THE COLUMN-BEST VALUE. system @5 @10 @20 ρ (test) AUC pair AST+VGGish 1.000 pair VGGish+MULE 1.000 triple AST+VGGish+MULE 1.000 1.000 SOTA [6] CLAP-text [10] random (chance) 0. 1.000 1.000 0.833 1.000 0.500 1.000 1.000 0.875 1.000 0.500 0.693 0.663 0.663 0.645 0.122 0. 0.468 0.478 0.510 0.477 0.484 0.500 vs-distractor ROC-AUC. Neither ρ nor AUC uses distractor labels, so the evaluation is not confounded by SOTAs weaklabel training data [6] (random baselines: @5 0.50, AUC 0.5, ρ 0). Table yields two findings. First, taste-space retrieval far outperforms text-space: every 5-D predictor saturates @k wherever defined, whereas CLAP-texts ρ (0.122) and AUC (0.484) sit at chance, surfacing taste-relevant item for only one of five queries, replicating the text-vs-attribute gap of [35]. Second, the regression-best fusion is not the rankbest: since precision cannot separate the predictors we read ρ, on which AST+VGGISH ranks most faithfully (0.693), edging VGGISH+MULE and the triple (both 0.663) and SOTA (0.645 [6]). So the model to deploy depends on the task: single VGGISH for low-error rating, AST+VGGISH for indexing. b) Per-query variance.: Per-query ρ for the best fusion spans 0.474 (citrus) to 0.820 (dark-chocolate), highest on sweet/bitter-dominant queries, consistent with their larger crossmodal effects [1], [2], [36]. AUC is near chance by construction, since the distractors share the test items [8] predicted-taste distribution, so the test-only ρ is the loadbearing metric, haystack-invariant when the distractors are swapped for 5,000 OOD FMA chunks [20]. VI. CONCLUSION We formalised taste-from-audio prediction as contentbased MIR benchmark over perceptually validated corpus [8]: frozen-encoder protocol over ten encoders across the four HEAR families [9], source-aware reporting against inter-rater ceilings, retrieval over 309-item pool, and psychophysics-grounded interpretability layer. Read for rating replacement, the best systems predict the five tastes within macro RMSE of 0.134; on held-out real music their error is less than half single raters deviation from the consensus (RMSE 0.13 vs. 0.28), so frozen-encoder model estimates the group rating more closely than an average human, and single VGGISH reaches it. The ablation (Table II) shows this gain over the prior baseline comes from loss and output redesign, not architecture; gated late-fusion adds only rank correlation (macro 0.724 vs. 0.666), useful for the indexing use case where generic CLAP-text [10] baseline collapses to chance. Because the predicted taste vector has naturallanguage reading, it can supply content-based recommendation [39], [40] with an interpretable axis. Limitations. The evaluation is small (269 training, 40 test clips, = 20 per source), which bounds generalisability and favours simple heads (the ridge probe nearly the matches the MLP); scaling the annotated corpus, not model, literature we is the main lever. The crossmodal lean on is Western and sweet/bitter-centric, so cross-cultural generalisation [7] and the under-constrained spicy/trigeminal axis [36] are open. Code and trained heads are at https: //github.com/CSCPadova/wav2taste; corpus is public at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ csc-unipd/sonic-seasoning. the
